metadata.title: TSP — Cryptographic proof for AI outputs.
metadata.description: Open protocol that wraps AI output as a signed TrustEnvelope: source declaration, process log, content hash, ledger hash, signature and independent verification.

header.complianceIsoDesc: Technical evidence support for ISO 42001 work.
header.devPlaygroundDesc: Sign and tamper with an envelope.

footer.tagline: Open specification and reference SDK for cryptographically verifiable AI provenance. Protocol, SDK, TrustBadge and hosted backends that help produce AI Act evidence. Built by a small team that believes trust in AI must be engineered in — not advertised.
footer.stamp: Built by a team that signs its work
footer.rightsLine: Spec, SDK and TrustBadge are free · Risk, Evidence and Oversight are paid tools · <specLink>Read the spec</specLink>

moduleLayout.fullStackDesc: Modules mapped article-by-article to the EU AI Act.

core.name: Core
core.tagline: Signs every AI answer so you can prove where it came from and that it hasn't been altered.

risk.name: Risk
risk.tagline: Watches AI answers in real time and alerts you when something drifts — before anyone complains.

oversight.name: Oversight
oversight.tagline: When an AI answer needs human review before it goes out — Oversight is the queue, the approval, and the signature.

evidence.name: Evidence
evidence.tagline: Packages your month into one document for the auditor — mapped to EU AI Act and ISO 42001 evidence work.
evidence.pricing: Paid license, usually bundled with Risk.

spec.metaTitle: TSP Spec v3.0 — Trust Standard Protocol
spec.metaDesc: Formal specification: Ed25519 signatures, RFC 3161 timestamps, manifest-PKI, DANE and hash chain.
spec.lead: A language-agnostic standard for cryptographically verifiable AI provenance. v3.0 adds Ed25519 signatures, RFC 3161 timestamps, manifest-based PKI with org-root and instance certs, and optional DNS DANE binding.

campaignAugust2.metaTitle: Are you ready for 2 August? — TSP AI Act evidence campaign
campaignAugust2.metaDesc: A hard but precise EU AI Act campaign: the missing infrastructure is a signed, inspectable receipt for what AI systems actually did.
campaignAugust2.lead: The AI Act did not create the trust problem. It exposed it. Your AI system will not be trusted because it says it is compliant; it will be trusted when every answer carries a signed, inspectable receipt. TSP turns each AI output into that TrustEnvelope: source, process, timestamp, signature and integrity checks.
campaignAugust2.urgencyBody: 2 August remains the Article 50 transparency pressure marker, not a lazy one-date story. On 7 May 2026, the Council and Parliament political agreement moved many high-risk application dates to 2 December 2027 and 2 August 2028 because support tools, standards and guidance still need to exist. That delay is the point: the market lacks operational evidence infrastructure.
campaignAugust2.sourceNote: Timeline source notes, kept deliberately narrow.
campaignAugust2.fact1Body: When rules need more time because standards, tools and guidance are not ready, the lesson is not to relax. It is to start producing runtime evidence before the audit and procurement window opens.
campaignAugust2.fact2Body: Policies describe intent. Runtime evidence shows which source, model, policy and timestamp produced the AI output people actually saw.
campaignAugust2.fact3Body: Article 99 sets material upper limits for infringements. The sane response is not panic. It is building evidence while systems run.
campaignAugust2.problemBody: Most teams can write a policy. The hard part is proving, months later, what an AI system produced, which source and model were involved, who reviewed it, and whether the output changed after signing. A PDF cannot answer that. A signed receipt can.
campaignAugust2.commercialBody: For vendors, deployers and public-sector suppliers, the first useful step is concrete: wrap one AI workflow, sign every answer, and let compliance inspect the receipt instead of screenshots, private logs or a dashboard screenshot.
campaignAugust2.policyTitle: A serious candidate for the de-facto evidence layer.
campaignAugust2.policyBody: The EU has rules. Enforcement needs a common evidence artifact. TSP proposes the open TrustEnvelope as that narrow protocol layer: neutral enough for procurement, technical enough for independent checking, and not a substitute for law, standards or conformity assessment.
campaignAugust2.proofTitle: The missing object is a signed, inspectable receipt.
campaignAugust2.proof1: Source declaration: what the answer relied on, not a vague claim that sources existed.
campaignAugust2.proof2: Process log: model, version, temperature, context window and policy metadata where available.
campaignAugust2.proof3: Integrity check: Ed25519 signatures, SHA-256 hashes and RFC 3161 timestamp evidence.
campaignAugust2.proof4: Independent verification: anyone can check the receipt without trusting a vendor dashboard.
campaignAugust2.proof5: Operational path: free SDK and TrustBadge first; Risk, Evidence and Oversight pilots when teams want the tools handled.
campaignAugust2.freeBody: The spec, SDK, TrustBadge, playground and local verification path are the adoption layer. Risk, Evidence and Oversight are optional paid pilot tools for teams that want the operational layer ready-made.
campaignAugust2.checklistTitle: One workflow. One receipt. One evidence pack.
campaignAugust2.checklistLead: The first campaign conversion should be concrete enough for a compliance officer to inspect and small enough for a developer to ship.
campaignAugust2.checklist1: Choose one AI workflow that affects a user, customer, employee or public decision.
campaignAugust2.checklist2: Sign one real output as a TrustEnvelope with source, process, timestamp and integrity evidence.
campaignAugust2.checklist3: Run the tamper test and show that a later edit breaks verification.
campaignAugust2.checklist4: Add TrustBadge so a non-technical reviewer can inspect the receipt.
campaignAugust2.checklist5: Package the signed artifacts as the first evidence pack.
campaignAugust2.procurementBody: Supplier shall provide TSP-compatible TrustEnvelopes, or an equivalent independently verifiable signed evidence artifact, for AI outputs in scope.
campaignAugust2.planTitle: Four weeks from deadline anxiety to working evidence.
campaignAugust2.planLead: The campaign should move prospects from awareness into a signed pilot artifact, while giving policy audiences a precise protocol proposal they can evaluate.
campaignAugust2.week1Body: Publish the landing page, founder post and readiness checklist. Send direct outreach to AI vendors, compliance leads and public-sector suppliers.
campaignAugust2.week2Body: Publish the technical proof post: model cards are not runtime evidence. Push the playground and tamper demo.
campaignAugust2.week3Body: Send the memo to EU-adjacent policy, standards and procurement audiences with the narrow TrustEnvelope protocol frame.
campaignAugust2.week4Body: Offer a scoped pilot: install SDK, wrap one workflow, produce signed artifacts and generate an evidence pack.
campaignAugust2.assetsLead: Use the commercial package for demos and pilot pipeline. Use the policy package when the audience needs the bigger argument: if not a neutral signed receipt, what is your alternative?
campaignAugust2.guardrail1: TSP is an evidence layer, not a legal certificate.
campaignAugust2.guardrail2: The standard starts free; the operational tools are paid pilot software.
campaignAugust2.guardrail3: The campaign cites 2 August 2026 without flattening the staged AI Act timeline.
campaignAugust2.guardrail4: TSP complements lawyers, notified bodies, QMS and data governance work.
campaignAugust2.finalTitle: The serious question is not why use TSP.
campaignAugust2.finalLead: The serious question is: what is your alternative? Start with one AI workflow. Sign the answer, break the signature, inspect the receipt, then decide whether to build the rest yourself or run a pilot.

translationBanner.title: Translation in progress
translationBanner.body: The hero and key sections are translated; the detailed spec content below remains in Norwegian for now. Full English translation is rolling out section by section.

articleLayout.helpBody: Want a TSP ↔ AI Act mapping tailored to your deployment?

euAiAct.metaTitle: EU AI Act — signed runtime evidence for AI outputs
euAiAct.metaDesc: TSP supports EU AI Act evidence work for articles 9, 12, 13, 14, 15, 17 and 50 through signed runtime artifacts.
euAiAct.h1: The EU AI Act turns AI trust into an evidence problem.
euAiAct.lead: The EU AI Act was written by lawyers, not developers — but articles 9, 12, 13, 14, 15, 17 and 50 create technical evidence questions. TSP supports those workstreams with signed runtime artifacts that are documented, open and independently checkable.
euAiAct.simpleTitle: The EU AI Act in three sentences — without jargon
euAiAct.simple1Body: If you ship AI that affects people's lives — in employment, health, welfare, education, justice — you are most likely an "Annex III high-risk system". That's most public-sector AI.
euAiAct.simple2Body: That the AI is safe, cryptographically checkable where signed, has human oversight, and that you have documentation to prove the process — continuously, not just at launch. Concretely: articles 9, 12, 13, 14, 15 and 17.
euAiAct.simple3Body: Produces technical evidence while your AI runs. Every response can be signed with source, process and risk markings. You get a stronger audit trail instead of relying only on a binder of papers that's outdated within a week.
euAiAct.simpleOutro: In other words: TSP does not replace your conformity work. It makes the technical proof objects easier to inspect.
euAiAct.claimP1After: . TSP is the runtime evidence layer that compliance, procurement and audit teams can inspect.
euAiAct.claimP2: We deliberately distinguish technical evidence (logging, transparency, integrity — supportable by software) from organizational compliance (policy, accountability, process — requires humans and decisions). TSP reduces the first gap. The second we cannot and should not solve — but we can make it cheaper to execute.
euAiAct.matrixLead: Click an article to see the formal text, the TSP solution and the artifacts generated.
euAiAct.art12Desc: Automatic logging with integrity over the system's lifetime.
euAiAct.art13Desc: Users must understand the AI system's purpose, limitations and what it can and cannot do.
euAiAct.art14Desc: Human supervision with the ability to intervene, override or stop the system.
euAiAct.art15Desc: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity proven technically.
euAiAct.art9Desc: Continuous risk management with detection and mitigation.
euAiAct.art17Desc: Holistic QMS — combines technical and organizational controls.
euAiAct.honestLead: We are deliberately clear about the limits. Positioning as a runtime evidence layer is narrow on purpose — not a silver bullet, not a legal certificate, but a concrete technical proof object.
euAiAct.notCovered1Desc: TSP logs source confidence at runtime, but training-time data governance (bias evaluation in datasets, representativeness) is out of scope.
euAiAct.notCovered1Suggestion: Integration partner: Collibra, Alation, or dedicated data governance platforms.
euAiAct.notCovered2Desc: TSP is an evidence provider, not the assessor. The process toward a notified body is legal-organizational.
euAiAct.notCovered2Suggestion: TSP Evidence packages everything into audit-ready dossiers — so the process takes days, not months.
euAiAct.notCovered3Desc: Article 17 requires a living quality system — meetings, decisions, accountable roles. That's human work.
euAiAct.notCovered3Suggestion: TSP Evidence is the backbone. ISO 42001 certification covers the rest.
euAiAct.ctaH2: Try the simplest proof chain first.
euAiAct.ctaLead: The playground shows the core: sign an AI answer, change one character, and watch verification break. That is the whole TSP story in five minutes.

iso42001.metaDesc: Clause-by-clause mapping between ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System) and TSP Evidence.
iso42001.lead: ISO/IEC 42001 (published 2023) defines an AI Management System (AIMS). TSP Evidence can be a technical evidence layer for that system — it does not cover everything, but it can reduce the digital evidence gap.
iso42001.strategyP1Strong: Build a QMS to ISO 42001, use TSP as technical evidence support, then assess the remaining organizational controls.
iso42001.strategyP1: TSP supports the evidence side of Article 17 work; certification still requires the full management system and external review.
iso42001.ctaH2: Discuss TSP Evidence for your ISO certification.
iso42001.ctaLead: We review your planned AIMS against TSP's mapping and identify which part of the documentation can be auto-generated.

priser.metaDesc: TSP Core is free and open. Risk, Evidence and Oversight are commercial tools with pilot pricing.
priser.h1: Free standard. Paid tools for ease of use.
priser.leadAfter: ) is MIT — free forever, sovereign-by-default. The convenience layer (Risk, Evidence and Oversight) is commercial: paid licenses for teams that want the operational tools instead of building them themselves.
priser.tier1F4: Your own manifest, signing key, infrastructure
priser.tier1F6: Risk alerts / Evidence dossiers / Oversight queue
priser.tier1F7: SLA and support from the team
priser.tier2F2: Risk module alpha (ingest, aggregate signals, signed alerts, webhooks)
priser.tier2F3: Evidence module alpha (signed dossiers, ISO 42001 and AI Act templates)
priser.tier2F4: Oversight module alpha (review-item API, signed verdicts, Risk webhook intake)
priser.tier2F5: Standalone hosted or on-prem deployment for Risk, Evidence and Oversight
priser.tier3F2: EU-region host (KS Sky, Bouvet, or your own)
priser.tier3F5: DPIA template for Annex III systems
priser.tier3F7: SLA terms by agreement after pilot hardening
priser.rolesLead: Three common situations. If one of them sounds like you, you already know what to choose.
priser.role1Scenario: "I'm a developer. I just need to show the compliance team we have a plan, but I don't want vendor lock-in."
priser.role1Reason: You install @lexitsp/sdk, wrap your AI calls with wrap(), and keep moving. Free, MIT, no us.
priser.role2Scenario: "I own compliance. We have certification in six months. I need something that actually works in production, not a PDF."
priser.role2Reason: Risk alerts when something drifts. Evidence packages the month for the auditor. Oversight gives signed approvals. Paid pilot pricing locks in for 3-6 months.
priser.role3Scenario: "I work in the public sector and have to clear regulators, the data protection authority and an internal audit."
priser.role3Reason: EU-region hosting, GDPR assessment included, DPIA template for Annex III, regulator coordination. Framework agreement, 24/7 SLA.
priser.honestTitle: Honest about where we are now
priser.honestLead: TSP is young. We're in pilot phase with selected customers on the way to stable v3.0. That means:
priser.honest1: locks in for the first 3–6 months. You pay the same price after we exit pilot.
priser.honest2: The standard zone is open source. If we do not deliver on the tools, your envelopes and verification chain remain yours.
priser.honest3: If your use case isn't a fit for TSP, we'll tell you immediately. No point in wasting budget on the wrong tool.
priser.incl1Body: Every envelope is signed with Ed25519 and timestamped by an RFC 3161 TSA. Tampering breaks mathematically.
priser.incl2Body: Paid tools can be LexiCo-hosted in the EU or licensed on-prem. The standard itself runs wherever you choose.
priser.incl3Body: Every module implements TSP Spec v3.0 — same protocol, no vendor lock.
priser.incl4Body: Full compliance mapping in Norwegian for Norwegian regulators; English for everyone else.
priser.incl5Body: The spec (CC-BY 4.0) and the standard zone (MIT) are freely available. You own what you build against.
priser.incl6Body: The TSP site has no Google Analytics, no Facebook pixel. Done.
priser.ctaH2: We send a concrete offer, not a sales deck.
priser.ctaLead: Tell us about your use case and volume, and you'll have numbers in hand within 24 hours.
priser.splitTitle: Adoption is free. Convenience is paid.
priser.splitLead: TSP should become infrastructure, not a toll booth. The standard layer spreads freely; the tools layer is where teams pay for speed, workflows and support.
priser.splitStandardBody: Spec, SDK, TrustBadge, manifests and local verification. Build your own implementation without asking LexiCo for permission.
priser.splitToolsBody: Risk, Evidence and Oversight. License them when you want the operational layer ready-made.
priser.splitRule: No LexiCo token. No phone-home. Commercial use is contractual, while the software stays standalone.

sammenligning.metaDesc: Honest comparison. When TSP is the right tool — and when it isn't.
sammenligning.lead: We believe in honest positioning. If you just need a policy PDF, TSP is overkill. If you want to build it yourself, DIY is cheaper on paper. The comparison below shows where TSP actually fits best.
sammenligning.takeChooseTspBody: You ship high-risk AI that actually needs to pass the EU AI Act in production. You want a chain-signed audit trail without writing it yourself. You prefer EU-region hosting.
sammenligning.takeChooseDiyBody: You have a large engineering team, no deadline, and building is the goal. You want an opinion on every data point. You don't want dependencies.
sammenligning.takeChooseConsultantBody: You need a conformity assessment, not runtime infrastructure. You have a low-risk system that just needs documentation before launch. You want hand-in-hand process with a notified body.
sammenligning.recoH2: If we were you, we'd probably combine.
sammenligning.recoP1: Most serious compliance projects need two things at once: runtime infrastructure (TSP or built yourself) for what the articles require of the code, and a consultant or lawyer for what the articles require of the organization. TSP and a good lawyer are complementary, not competing.
sammenligning.recoP2: The most common mistake we see: buying a thick policy PDF and believing art. 12 is solved. It never is.
sammenligning.rowSignNote: DIY solutions usually only log, without signatures. Governance platforms often include audit logging, but not chain-secure.
sammenligning.rowArt12: EU AI Act art. 12 mapping out of the box
sammenligning.rowVerify: Third party can verify mathematically without asking the vendor
sammenligning.rowEu: EU-region host + GDPR conformity by default
sammenligning.rowSpec: Open spec + MIT implementation (no vendor lock-in)
sammenligning.rowAssessment: Conformity assessment process with a notified body
sammenligning.rowAssessmentNote: Notified-body process. TSP provides documentation, not the assessment itself.

kontakt.metaDesc: Talk to the team behind TSP. Pilots for compliance teams, integration for developers, demos for procurement.
kontakt.leadBefore: TSP is still young, and honest about it. We answer fast, and we'll tell you straight whether TSP is the right tool for your situation. Send a short note to
kontakt.buyerBody: You're procuring AI for the public sector and need to demonstrate EU AI Act compliance. We'll show you a pilot on your own use case.
kontakt.buyerSubject: Demo / pilot — TSP for the public sector
kontakt.buyerEmailBody:
Hi,

I'm evaluating TSP for a compliance project and would like to see a demo / pilot on our use case.

Our situation:
- Sector: 
- Use case: 
- Timeline: 

Thanks
kontakt.complianceBody: You own ISO 42001 or EU AI Act documentation. We talk specific articles — Art. 12, 14, 15, 17 — and what TSP solves where.
kontakt.complianceEmailBody:
Hi,

I work in compliance and have questions about how TSP addresses:
- EU AI Act art. 
- ISO 42001 

Thanks
kontakt.devBody: You're integrating TSP into an existing AI stack. We'll give you SDK access, examples, and onboarding support for the first week.
kontakt.devEmailBody:
Hi,

I want to integrate TSP into:
- Stack: 
- Volume (envelopes/day): 
- Model: 

Thanks
kontakt.emailDesc: We answer within one business day, often sooner.
kontakt.playgroundDesc: 5 minutes in the verifier shows the proof loop before anyone spends time in a meeting.
kontakt.expect1: We'll tell you immediately whether TSP is right for your situation.
kontakt.expect2: where we understand the use case and show how TSP solves exactly what you need — not a generic sales pitch.
kontakt.expect3: If it's a fit, we run a scoped pilot on your use case or provide SDK + onboarding support.

whitepaper.metaTitle: Whitepaper — being rewritten for v3 · TSP
whitepaper.metaDesc: The whitepaper is being rewritten for TSP v3.0. Until then — the spec, the AI Act articles, and LexiCo directly.
whitepaper.h1: The whitepaper is being rewritten for v3.
whitepaper.leadP1: The whitepaper is being rewritten for TSP v3 as a standalone standard. The old v0.1 PDF mixed the protocol with a shelved reference project, so it has been removed from the main flow.
whitepaper.leadP2: Rather than leaving an inaccurate PDF up while we rewrite, we point to sources that are actually correct right now:
whitepaper.card1Desc: The authoritative technical documentation. Schema, JCS canonicalization, the chain mechanism, PII patterns, governance.
whitepaper.card2Desc: Article by article: how TSP covers (or doesn't cover) Art. 9, 12, 13, 14, 15 and 17. Each mapping has concrete envelope fields.
whitepaper.card3Desc: What a 30-day TSP pilot looks like: installation, signed artifacts, evidence package and decision basis.
whitepaper.card4Desc: Need the whitepaper as a reference in a procurement process before the rewrite is done? Send us an email — we'll send a draft.
whitepaper.honestQ: Why didn't you just update the PDF?
whitepaper.honestA: We believe language is architecture. A PDF that claims things the code does not do is worse than no PDF. We will publish a narrower whitepaper when v3.0.0-beta is stable and can be documented with artifacts, not ambitions.

endringer.metaDesc: What's new in TSP. Version log and what's coming next.
endringer.h1: What we've built — and what's coming.
endringer.lead: An open version log. We tell you what's new, what's fixed, what's breaking. Plus what's coming, with an honest timeline.
endringer.roadmapLead: We prioritize brutally — and are honest when something slips.
endringer.honestBody: We are a small team. The release pace is aggressive because TSP is still alpha and needs to become easier to adopt. If you run TSP in production, let us know, and we promise early warning on breaking changes.

moduleOversight.metaDesc: Platform module. Signed ReviewEnvelopes with client-side signing. The server never holds reviewer keys. No AI-suggested guidance — the type system forbids it.
moduleOversight.practiceP1: Oversight provides the backend for human review: review-item queue APIs, claim/release, signed reviewer decisions, and Risk-alarm webhook intake. It is not yet a full reviewer portal product on its own.
moduleOversight.practiceP2: EU AI Act art. 14 requires that humans have genuine ability to intervene. Oversight gives reviewers a three-state verdict (approved / approved-with-concerns / rejected) which is signed client-side with the reviewer's own Ed25519 key. The result is a ReviewEnvelope (tsp-review-1.0). The server never gives the reviewer LLM-generated suggestions, summaries or "tentative assessments" — the type system forbids it. The human decides, not the AI.
moduleOversight.licenseSummary: Commercial license. SDK + TrustBadge stay MIT; the Oversight server and portal are paid tools.
moduleOversight.licenseDetail1: The Oversight server and review portal require a written commercial agreement for hosted or on-prem use.
moduleOversight.licenseDetail2: @lexitsp/trustbadge-react@0.2.2 remains MIT: the visible receipt should spread with the standard.
moduleOversight.licenseDetail3: Reviewer keys generated locally (WebCrypto extractable=false) — server never holds them.
moduleOversight.licenseDetail4: Triggers: Risk alerts (auto) + customer-written deterministic rules + manual. No AI pre-filtering.
moduleOversight.componentsTitle: What Oversight delivers to the UI
moduleOversight.feature1Desc: Clear, color-coded indicator showing the confidence level (high/medium/low/critical). Drop-in next to every AI answer. 4 lines of integration.
moduleOversight.feature2Desc: Opens on click of the TrustBadge. Shows all 6 points from AI Act Art. 13(3)(b) in plain language. Source, legal basis, process details, risk assessment.
moduleOversight.feature3Desc: Backend queue for envelopes with humanReviewRequired=true, including claim/release/decide endpoints. Team assignment and SLA UI still need productization.
moduleOversight.feature4Desc: Reviewer decisions are signed and stored as ReviewEnvelopes. Editing/override UX is not yet a complete shipped interface.
moduleOversight.feature5Desc: Deterministic queue-rule primitives exist. YAML authoring UI, SLA timers and escalation UX are planned product work.
moduleOversight.feature6Desc: Automation-bias interrupts are a TrustBadge/Oversight product direction. The current server enforces no-AI-suggestion constraints, but active interrupt UX still needs implementation.
moduleOversight.integrationTitle: How to add Oversight to your app
moduleOversight.integrationIntro: TrustBadge + TrustModal live in one component package. Three lines to display, three lines to send an envelope to the reviewer queue.
moduleOversight.codeReact: React component in the chat UI
moduleOversight.codeDecision: Reviewer decision written to the same chain
moduleOversight.coverageArt14: Human oversight — components + reviewer queue + override interface.
moduleOversight.coverageArt13: Transparency — TrustBadge and TrustModal render envelope metadata.
moduleOversight.coverageArt9: Risk management — human reviews are themselves the mitigation mechanism for high risk.
moduleOversight.integrationP1: A typical TSP deployment shows TrustBadge next to every AI answer in the product. One click opens a panel with source declaration, status, model information and what the user should do next.
moduleOversight.integrationP2: The reviewer flow should be configured against alignment.flags, alignment.refusal and deterministic queue rules — no AI-suggested routing.
moduleOversight.integrationP3: Actual flag rates and review-outcome distributions belong in each customer evidence package, not in TSP marketing.

moduleEvidence.metaDesc: Automatic generation of audit documentation mapped to the EU AI Act and ISO 42001. Covers Art. 17.
moduleEvidence.practiceP1: Evidence can generate signed compliance dossiers from verified envelopes, Risk aggregates and templates. In this standalone alpha, the template engine and job API are working; production source lookup must still be wired to the customer ledger and Risk instance.
moduleEvidence.practiceP2: Evidence produces audit-ready dossiers — pre-structured documentation the compliance officer can send directly to the certification auditor. Mapping to EU AI Act art. 17 and ISO 42001 is automatic and reproducible.
moduleEvidence.licenseSummary: Commercial license. Usually bundled with Risk in the TSP Tools package.
moduleEvidence.licenseDetail1: Annual commercial license. Separate SKU or bundled with Risk/Oversight.
moduleEvidence.licenseDetail2: Includes all ISO 42001 clause mappings and Annex IV templates.
moduleEvidence.licenseDetail3: Source code available for internal audit — the dossier format must be verifiable.
moduleEvidence.licenseDetail4: Updates when standards change (ISO revisions, AI Act delegated acts).
moduleEvidence.feature1Desc: Pre-structured signed dossier with AI Act mapping, chain integrity, statistics and attestations. The alpha currently emits JSON/Markdown-backed evidence envelopes; polished PDF export is still product work.
moduleEvidence.feature2Desc: Each envelope field mapped to the relevant ISO 42001 clauses (7.3, 8.1, 9.1, etc.). Reproducible and auditable.
moduleEvidence.feature3Desc: The job/schedule API exists. Fully automated monthly distribution to board and management is planned workflow, not finished product behavior.
moduleEvidence.feature4Desc: Conformity Assessment documentation in a format that aligns with AI Act Annex IV. Fills in every field that can be auto-filled.
moduleEvidence.feature5Desc: Mathematical proof of ledger integrity included in every dossier. The auditor can re-verify with a single command.
moduleEvidence.feature6Desc: Attestation sections can be linked through documentation envelopes. A complete role-signing workflow for CTO/CCO attestations is still to be built.
moduleEvidence.anatomyTitle: What a finished report looks like
moduleEvidence.anatomyIntro: Every dossier has the same overall structure. Content varies by deployment, but the mapping logic is standardized and reproducible.
moduleEvidence.coverageArt17: Quality management — technical backbone for QMS documentation.
moduleEvidence.coverageArt12: Record-keeping — dossiers export ledger excerpts in structured format.
moduleEvidence.coverageArt9: Risk management — aggregates from the Risk module flow into the dossier.
moduleEvidence.coverageArt15: Accuracy & robustness — chain-integrity proof is included in every dossier.
moduleEvidence.integrationP1: Evidence is designed to generate monthly reports the compliance officer can review and sign in a few hours — versus weeks of manual QMS documentation.
moduleEvidence.integrationP2: The pilot hypothesis is simple: ISO 42001 on a TSP foundation should cost a fraction of the equivalent process without signed runtime artifacts. The number must be measured per customer.
moduleEvidence.integrationP3: The dossier format is designed to be delivered directly to the certification auditor as part of stage 1 audit. The mapping between envelope fields and ISO 42001 clauses is consistent and reproducible.

moduleRisk.metaTitle: TSP Risk — envelope stream aggregation · TSP
moduleRisk.metaDesc: Platform module. Drift detection over the envelope stream, signed alert envelopes with Merkle-bound drivers. Covers EU AI Act Art. 9. Aggregates at envelope level, never per user.
moduleRisk.practiceP1: Risk ingests signed AI-answer envelopes, verifies them, aggregates envelope-level signals, and creates signed alerts when configured thresholds break. It is useful as a backend monitoring primitive today; the polished dashboard and third-party alert integrations are still product work.
moduleRisk.practiceP2: Risk reads the envelope stream (signed dual-write from the SDK or direct POST) and computes deterministic aggregate statistics per (model, signal, day). When a threshold breaks, a signed RiskEnvelope (tsp-risk-1.0) is published with a Merkle root over the envelopes that drove the signal. Aggregation happens across envelopes, never across users — that's a hard boundary, not a preference.
moduleRisk.licenseSummary: Commercial license. Source available for licensed customers and audits; not free to run as a service.
moduleRisk.licenseDetail1: The Risk server requires a written commercial agreement for hosted or on-prem use.
moduleRisk.licenseDetail2: verifyOnline() per envelope on ingest — the verification outcome is itself a first-class signal.
moduleRisk.licenseDetail3: Fixed, customer-configurable thresholds. No AI interpretation of numbers — only deterministic rules.
moduleRisk.licenseDetail4: Storage: 13 months of aggregate buckets, 90 days of ID pointers, 7 years of signed alarms. Never the content — only the signature.
moduleRisk.feature1Desc: Envelope-level buckets exposed through the API. The current site preview is synthetic; production dashboards still need customer UI work.
moduleRisk.feature2Desc: Rolling average on biasScore per domain/model version. Alert when the distribution shifts significantly after a model or prompt change.
moduleRisk.feature3Desc: Threshold config for deterministic alert rules and signed alert output. Direct Slack/JIRA/PagerDuty adapters should be treated as planned integrations, not current shipped connectors.
moduleRisk.feature4Desc: Automatic grouping of alignment.flags. Spots patterns in 'irreversible-action' or 'high-stakes' before an auditor does.
moduleRisk.feature5Desc: Signal data and signed alerts can be exported to Evidence; polished PDF reporting is still product work.
moduleRisk.feature6Desc: Alerts can be delivered by webhook and tracked externally. A full incident-management workflow inside TSP is not shipped yet.
moduleRisk.alertsTitle: How bias drift is actually caught
moduleRisk.alertsIntro: Scenario: you switch from GPT-4 to GPT-4o in your HR chatbot. The model is 15 % faster, but unknown: whether it produces more or less biased answers against names from minority groups. TSP Risk is ready.
moduleRisk.coverageArt9: Risk management — a continuous iterative process across the full lifecycle.
moduleRisk.coverageArt15: Accuracy & robustness — monitors confidence distribution and detects performance changes.
moduleRisk.coverageArt17: Quality management — post-market monitoring is directly part of the QMS requirements.
moduleRisk.previewDesc: Interactive mock of the TSP Risk dashboard based on a synthetic 30-day envelope stream. It shows the intended product surface; the backend APIs already provide the underlying signal data.
moduleRisk.integrationP1: A typical TSP Risk deployment starts with these alert rules:
moduleRisk.integrationRule1: alignment.refusal is set and alignment.humanReviewRequired=false — should be 0 in high-risk domains.
moduleRisk.integrationRule2: The share of envelopes with declaration.primarySource.provided=false rises above the policy threshold.
moduleRisk.integrationRule3: New process.systemPrompt.hash values in production — catches unintended prompt changes that slipped through CI/CD.
moduleRisk.integrationRule4: Envelopes with the wrong alignment.policy.id in a regulated domain signal incorrect upstream routing.
moduleRisk.integrationP2: Alert volume and false-positive rate should be published by each deployment as part of its own evidence packages.

moduleCore.metaDesc: TSP Core is the protocol. Envelope structure, hash chain, verify. Open standard under MIT license.
moduleCore.practiceIntroP1: Whenever your AI answers, Core creates a signed receipt. The receipt states exactly which source the answer is built on, which model produced it, and when. If anyone later changes a single character, the signature breaks — and you see it immediately.
moduleCore.practiceIntroP2: TSP Core is the protocol foundation — it defines the TrustEnvelope structure, RFC 8785 canonicalization, SHA-256 hashing and Ed25519 signing. It's the standard — in the same spirit as HTTP or TLS.
moduleCore.licenseSummary: Open standard. No license fees. No vendor lock-in.
moduleCore.licenseDetail1: Implement it yourself in any language — the spec is public and versioned.
moduleCore.licenseDetail2: Host your own ledger — we never require data to be sent to us.
moduleCore.licenseDetail3: Forking is allowed, rebranding is allowed, commercial use is allowed.
moduleCore.feature1Desc: Deterministic JSON schema with four main fields: content, source, process, alignment. Plus ledger metadata. Versioned via JSON-LD @context.
moduleCore.feature2Desc: Each envelope is linked to the previous one via SHA-256. Tamper evidence is mathematical, not policy-based.
moduleCore.feature3Desc: Reproducible JSON serialization (sorted keys, normalized whitespace) so the hash is identical across languages and platforms.
moduleCore.feature4Desc: confidenceScore (0–100) computed from source × process × alignment with fixed weights. Same input, same score, every time.
moduleCore.feature5Desc: Any third party can run verifyChain() and get a mathematical answer on whether the chain is intact. No vendor trust required.
moduleCore.feature6Desc: Drop-in SDK for Node/TS today. Python, Go, .NET in development. Requires a single URL change.
moduleCore.quickstartTitle: How Core is used in practice
moduleCore.quickstartIntro: TSP Core is a proxy. Your app sends AI responses through TSP, which wraps them in an envelope, signs them, and links them to the previous envelope. The return value is the envelope — ready to display, store, and verify.
moduleCore.coverageArt12: Record-keeping — automatic logging with cryptographic integrity.
moduleCore.coverageArt13: Transparency — structured metadata fields in every envelope.
moduleCore.coverageArt15: Accuracy & robustness — deterministic scoring and tamper evidence.
moduleCore.coverageArt14: Human oversight — the humanReviewRequired flag triggers the Oversight module.
moduleCore.coverageArt9: Risk management — structured risk signals live in the alignment field.
moduleCore.integrationP1: A typical TSP deployment integrates @lexitsp/sdk/v3 behind every AI response. The integration pattern is a clean wrap() call around existing AI calls — the RAG pipeline and UI code do not need to be replaced.
moduleCore.integrationP2: Once the ledger is exposed, any third party can verify it via verifyLocal() or the CLI without asking the vendor.

hero.lead: TSP turns AI answers into independently verifiable TrustEnvelopes: <kilde>what was said</kilde>, <model>which source and process produced it</model>, and whether the bytes still match.
hero.leadTechnical: Think HTTPS-style verification for AI as an analogy: not a promise that the provider is good, but a way to inspect the proof. The open protocol and SDK are free; operational Risk, Evidence and Oversight tooling is optional.
hero.specCellComplianceValue: EU AI Act · ISO 42001 · RFC 8785 · RFC 3161

verificationGap.h2: AI trust is moving from assertion to proof.
verificationGap.lead: Policies, dashboards and PDFs can describe governance. They do not prove what a runtime AI system actually returned. TSP proposes a neutral, independently verifiable evidence artifact for that missing layer.
verificationGap.card1Body: The vendor says an AI answer was compliant, logged or reviewed. Everyone else has to trust the vendor's story.
verificationGap.card2Body: A TrustEnvelope carries source, process, timestamp, hash chain and signature with the output itself.
verificationGap.card3Body: A buyer, user, auditor or regulator can inspect the artifact and see whether it still holds.

manifest.claim: We claim only what the code actually does. The standard is open (CC-BY 4.0), the SDK and TrustBadge are MIT, and verification works without asking TSP or any SaaS vendor for permission. If we disappear, your implementation keeps working.

audience.h2: Three angles on the same problem.
audience.lead: The EU AI Act hits three roles differently — and all three meet TSP from a different entry point. Plain English first, technical depth where you need it.
audience.complianceConcern: You've been told the AI system must meet the EU AI Act. You're looking for something you can document — not yet another policy PDF.
audience.complianceWhat: TSP gives you a signed receipt for every AI answer. You can show the regulator exactly which source was used, which model produced the answer, and that nobody altered it afterwards. Mapped article-by-article to AI Act art. 9, 12, 13, 14 and 15.
audience.devConcern: You're going to build this yourself or integrate something. You don't want vendor lock-in, a black box, or to write 600 lines of cryptography from scratch.
audience.devWhat: @lexitsp/sdk is MIT, runs in Node, Bun and the browser, and uses only standard algorithms (Ed25519 + SHA-256 + RFC 3161). The spec is open — you can reimplement it in any language.
audience.leaderConcern: You're choosing infrastructure your team will build on for five years. You don't want to pay a SaaS vendor on a back-contract for something that should be a standard.
audience.leaderWhat: The standard zone (SDK + TrustBadge) is MIT forever. The convenience tools (Risk, Evidence and Oversight) are paid licenses for teams that want operations handled instead of rebuilding them.

standard.lead: Sovereign-by-default. MIT-licensed. Implemented in TypeScript as the reference, specified as structured JSON with RFC 8785 canonicalization.
standard.subLead: Run it where you want. Implement it in any language. We don't own your chain.
standard.moduleSdkDesc: The protocol implementation: TrustEnvelope construction, Ed25519, RFC 3161, manifest-PKI, DANE.
standard.moduleBadgeDesc: Visual receipt for end users — one click opens a panel with seven canonical fields.

platform.lead: Commercial modules for EU AI Act and ISO 42001 teams that want the operations layer ready-made. All return signed envelopes you can verify independently.
platform.subLead: Use the free standard directly, or license the tools when speed and support matter.
platform.moduleRiskDesc: Drift detection over the envelope stream with Merkle-bound alert envelopes. Aggregates at envelope level, never per user.
platform.moduleEvidenceDesc: Signed Annex IV + ISO 42001 §9.1 reports against the envelope ledger. Never a permanent archive.
platform.moduleOversightDesc: Human-in-the-loop with signed ReviewEnvelopes. Reviewer signatures happen client-side — the server never holds reviewer keys.

envelope.h2: Three regulator-relevant fields, one verifiable artifact.
envelope.lead: A TSP artifact is not free text claiming quality — it's a structured JSON object where every factual claim is measurable.
envelope.fieldDeclarationDesc: Structured pointer to primary source and cited paragraphs. The source must be declared, not assumed.
envelope.fieldProcessDesc: Model ID, version, temperature, context window, system prompt (or explicit redaction with reason).
envelope.fieldAlignmentDesc: Structured uncertainty flags, refusal reason, policy ID + version. No 'AI says it's safe' — only measurable fields.
envelope.fieldTimestampDesc: RFC 3161 TSA token. UUIDv7 + SHA-256 hash chain. Ed25519 signature over RFC 8785-canonicalized payload.
envelope.readFullSchema: Read the full schema in §3 of the spec

reference.h2: The proof is the receipt itself.
reference.p1: TSP stands alone as a protocol: TrustEnvelope, SDK, CLI, TrustBadge and optional operational modules. No external case is needed to understand the core.
reference.p2: The most important proof point is reproducible: sign an answer, change it, verify the break.

next.h2: Three concrete things to do now.
next.tryBody: Write an AI answer in your browser, sign it with TSP, see what happens when you try to alter it. Nothing to install.
next.compareBody: DIY logging, governance platforms, consultancy PDFs — what TSP does differently, and when TSP is the wrong tool.
next.contactBody: Concrete pricing within 24 hours. We'll say no if your use case doesn't fit — better than wasting budget on the wrong tool.

modules.lead: The standard zone is MIT-licensed and runs without us. The platform zone is optional commercial tooling for teams who want operations handled.
modules.subLead: Everything emits signed envelopes you can verify independently.
modules.moduleSdkDesc: TrustEnvelope construction, Ed25519, RFC 3161, manifest-PKI, DANE.
modules.moduleBadgeDesc: Visual receipt for end-users — one click reveals seven canonical fields.
modules.moduleRiskDesc: Drift detection over the envelope stream with Merkle-bound alerts.
modules.moduleEvidenceDesc: Signed Annex IV + ISO 42001 §9.1 reports against the ledger.
modules.moduleOversightDesc: Human-in-the-loop with signed ReviewEnvelopes. Reviewer keys stay client-side.

standing.col1Body: @lexitsp/sdk + @lexitsp/trustbadge-react — both MIT. W3C requires two interoperable implementations to promote a candidate spec; TSP ships both already.
standing.col2Body: Anyone can verify a TrustEnvelope with a public key and SHA-256. No API call, no rate limit, no dependency on us. If we disappear, every receipt still works.
standing.col3Body: Norwegian-built, EU-hosted, GDPR-native. No US cloud dependency in the verification path. Procurement-friendly for EU enterprise.

cliff.h2: The evidence cliff starts before enforcement does.
cliff.lead: 2 August 2026 remains the pressure marker for broad AI Act application and transparency. The new political agreement moves many high-risk dates later so standards and tools can exist. That is the signal: evidence infrastructure is still missing.
cliff.countdownLabel: Time until the August pressure marker
cliff.penaltyTopLabel: or 7% of global turnover · top-tier fine (Art. 99, prohibited practices)
cliff.penaltyMainLabel: or 3% of global turnover · selected operator and transparency infringements
cliff.calloutTitle: The delay is not comfort. It is evidence of the infrastructure gap.
cliff.calloutBody: The Council and Parliament agreement moves high-risk application dates so standards, support tools and guidance can exist. That is the market signal: policy can mandate evidence, but someone still has to ship the receipt.
cliff.calloutEmphasis: TSP is what evidence readiness can look like.

articleMap.h2: The Act asks for evidence. TSP turns evidence into an artifact.
articleMap.lead: The EU AI Act names record-keeping, transparency, oversight, robustness and documentation duties. TSP is not legal compliance by itself; it is the signed technical object those workstreams can inspect.
articleMap.row1Requires: Automatic logs of events over each system's lifetime
articleMap.row1Tsp: TrustEnvelope ledger · UUIDv7 + SHA-256 hash chain · immutable by construction
articleMap.row2Requires: Transparency to deployers · clear and complete information
articleMap.row2Tsp: declaration block · structured source pointer · model name + version + temperature
articleMap.row3Tsp: Oversight module · signed ReviewEnvelopes · reviewer keys client-side only
articleMap.row4Requires: Accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity · resilience against tampering
articleMap.row4Tsp: Ed25519 signature over RFC 8785-canonicalised payload · one character changed, verification fails
articleMap.row5Requires: Quality management system · documented procedures, records, reporting
articleMap.row5Tsp: Evidence module · signed Annex IV reports · ISO 42001 §9.1 mapping
articleMap.row6Requires: Transparency to natural persons · disclosure that they interact with AI
articleMap.row6Tsp: TrustBadge · one-click visual receipt · seven canonical fields any end-user can read
articleMap.footer: Six pressure points. Six concrete evidence artifacts. No PDF-only posture. No "AI says it is safe". Every technical claim has something inspectable behind it.

trustFlip.h2: AIs without receipts should start to feel untrusted.
trustFlip.lead: Once a signed receipt format exists, the absence of one becomes a signal. The bet is simple: users, buyers and compliance teams will ask why an AI system cannot show its work.
trustFlip.withoutBody: Plain text. No declared source. No signature. No way to know which model said it, against which policy, or whether the bytes were altered afterwards.
trustFlip.withBody: Signed envelope. Declared source. RFC 3161 timestamp. Hash-chained to the previous answer. Anyone — regulator, customer, journalist — can verify offline in one click.
trustFlip.manifesto: In five years, an AI answer without a signed receipt should feel like an unsigned email from your bank. A future checkout page without HTTPS feels wrong; unverifiable AI should get there too.

noArgument.h2: The burden is now on the alternative.
noArgument.lead: The serious question is not why use TSP. The serious question is: what is your alternative when a buyer, auditor or user asks for a signed receipt that can survive inspection?
noArgument.col1Body: The SDK is MIT-licensed. €0 to test. A thin wrap around your existing AI call. Article 99 sets material penalty ceilings for selected infringements, so 'too expensive to try' is a weak answer.
noArgument.col2Body: The spec is open under CC-BY 4.0. Reference implementation is MIT. Implement it in any language. We host nothing of yours. If we disappear, your verification still works.
noArgument.col3Body: One integration pattern: wrap the output, publish the manifest, show the badge. Verification works locally, without trusting us. No new trust silo is required.
noArgument.footer: Default-test the open standard. Reject it only with a better neutral signed receipt format.
